slides.title: SQLite Architecture (Elite)
slides.subtitle:
Engineering the World's Most Deployed Database
Internal Mechanics, VFS, and Performance Engineering

box.content: The Zero-Configuration, Serverless Storage Engine.
A C-language library that implements a small, fast, self-contained, high-reliability, full-featured, SQL database engine.

list.text: Self-Contained: No external dependencies.
Serverless: No separate server process.
Single File: The entire database is a single disk file.

box.content: SQLite is found in nearly every modern electronic device.

list.text: Mobile: Android, iOS, and IoT.
Desktop: Browsers (Chrome, Firefox), macOS, Windows.
Automotive: Infotainment systems.

box.content: How SQLite processes a query.
A stack of layers from the Tokenizer to the OS interface.

list.text: Front-End: Tokenizer, Parser, Code Generator.
Back-End: B-Tree, Pager, VFS (Virtual File System).

box.content: SQLite's internal bytecode processor.
SQLite doesn't interpret SQL directly; it compiles it to bytecode.

list.text: Instructions: Opcode + P1, P2, P3, P4, P5 parameters.
Execution: The Virtual Machine runs this bytecode to fetch/modify data.

box.code: EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = 10;

box.content: The VDBE is a register-based VM (unlike JVM which is stack-based).

list.text: Efficiency: Less data movement between memory locations.

box.content: How data is organized on disk.
Organizes data into hierarchical pages for fast lookup.

list.text: Table B-Trees: Key is the RowID, data in leaves.
Index B-Trees: Key is the index value + RowID.

box.content: Manages reading/writing fixed-size pages (usually 4KB).

list.text: Caching: The pager keeps frequently used pages in memory.
Locking: Coordinates access between multiple processes.

box.content: How SQLite handles multi-process access.
Original Rollback Journal mechanism.

list.text: DELETE/TRUNCATE: Original pages are stored in a journal file.
Locking: Writers block all readers (Limited concurrency).

box.content: Write-Ahead Logging transforms performance.

list.text: Features: Readers don't block writers. Very fast commits.
Mechanism: Changes go to a -wal file; periodic checkpoints move them to the DB.

box.content: The -shm file coordinates connections in WAL mode.

list.text: Fast IPC: Uses mmap() for lightning-fast synchronization between processes.

box.content: The secret to SQLite's portability.
SQLite doesn't talk to the OS directly; it uses a VFS object.

list.text: Standard: unix-dotkf, unix-excl, win32-longpath.
Custom: You can write a VFS to store data in a ZIP file or across a network.

box.content: SQLite's unique 'Typeless' architecture.
In SQLite, the datatype is a property of the value itself, not the column.

list.text: Flexibility: You can store a string in an INTEGER column.
Storage: SQLite automatically uses the smallest storage class for each value.

box.content: For developers who want traditional rigid typing.
box.code: CREATE TABLE users (id INT PRIMARY KEY) STRICT;

box.content: Pro-level SQL features in 1MB of C code.
Solving graph problems and generating sequences.
High-performance JSON document support.
Computed values stored or calculated on-the-fly.
box.code: full_name TEXT GENERATED ALWAYS AS (first || ' ' || last)

box.content: Taking SQLite beyond a single node.
FUSE-based replication for SQLite.

list.text: Replication: Streams changes to replicas globally.

box.content: The SQLite fork designed for the cloud.

list.text: Serverless: HTTP interface, multi-tenancy.

box.content: Squeezing every drop of performance.

list.text: synchronous=OFF: Extreme speed (at risk of corruption).
cache_size: controlling the pager cache.
busy_timeout: Handling lock contention.

box.content: Reading the VDBE optimization paths.
box.code: EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN SELECT * FROM orders WHERE cid = 5;

box.content: Reclaiming space without locking the whole database.
box.code:
PRAGMA auto_vacuum = INCREMENTAL;
PRAGMA incremental_vacuum(100); # Reclaim up to 100 pages

box.content: Expanding the core with modular features.
Professional-grade search engine built into SQLite.
box.code: CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE docs USING fts5(title, body);

box.content: Efficient spatial queries for maps and sensors.
box.code: CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE spatial_idx USING rtree(id, minX, maxX, minY, maxY);

box.content: Extracting changesets for offline sync.

list.text: Changeset: Capture all changes made to a database.
Sync: Send the changeset to another node to merge.

box.content: Loading C-shared libraries at runtime.
Why SQLite is just two files.
All 100+ source files combined into one giant `sqlite3.c` file.

list.text: Benefits: Easier to embed. Compiler can optimize across the whole engine (Inter-procedural optimization).

box.content: Stripping features or enabling optimizations at compile time.
How SQLite protects your data.
Ensuring transactions are all-or-nothing even during power loss.

list.text: Commit Record: The single bit flip that marks a transaction as final.

box.content: How databases die (and how to avoid it).

list.text: Hardware: Disk failures or memory bit-flips.
User Error: Opening a database file in a text editor.
OS Bugs: Faulty file locking or sync implementations.

box.content: The famous SQLite test suite.

list.text: Coverage: 100% of the code is tested by the public test suite.

box.content: Keeping the file healthy.
Rebuilding the database to reclaim space and defragment.
Updating statistics for the query optimizer.
Pro-tips for the sqlite3 terminal.

list.text: .mode box: Beautifully formatted output tables.
.timer on: Measure execution time for every query.
.import: Fast CSV/TSV loading.

box.content: The engine that runs the world.

list.text: Reliability: 100% branch coverage tests.
Scalability: Up to 281 Terabytes.
Deployment: Everywhere.

box.content: Thank you! Any questions?
